nativeRange.habitat: A cultivar of hybrid origin that does not occur in the wild. The parent species inhabit shaded streams, riverbanks, and forest swamps in tropical West and Central Africa, where they grow epiphytically on submerged wood and rocks.

decorPreferences: shaded areas under taller plants or floating cover

summary: Anubias Nancon is a cultivar created by crossing Anubias barteri var. 'Nana' with Anubias congensis, producing a plant intermediate between its parents — taller and with longer, more pointed leaves than A. nana. It is slow-growing, undemanding, and well suited to the midground or background of a planted tank.
careNotes:
Anubias 'Nancon' is a man-made cultivar widely attributed to aquarist Bob Gasser, created by crossing Anubias barteri var. 'Nana' with Anubias congensis. In the aquarium trade the second parent is sometimes labelled as Anubias afzelii 'Congensis', an older name; both names refer to the same species, which is now treated as a synonym of A. heterophylla in current taxonomy. The trade name 'Nancon' is a contraction of the two parent epithets. The hybrid sits between its parents in form: more compact than A. congensis but taller and with longer, more lanceolate (lance-shaped) leaves than A. nana, with mature plants commonly reported in the 13–28 cm height range.

Husbandry follows the same principles as for other Anubias. The plant is epiphytic — meaning it grows attached to surfaces rather than rooted in soil — and the rhizome (the thick, creeping stem from which leaves and roots grow) MUST remain above the substrate. Attach it to driftwood, rocks, or other hardscape using cyanoacrylate glue, cotton thread, or fishing line, burying only the white roots. Burying the rhizome causes rot and plant death.

Lighting is generally low to medium. Because the cultivar is slow-growing, bright light tends to promote algae growth on the leaves rather than faster plant growth; positioning the plant in shade under floating or taller plants helps keep this in check. CO2 injection is not required, and Anubias are commonly reported as not responding strongly to supplemental carbon dioxide. The plant feeds primarily from the water column, so liquid fertilization tends to be more impactful than substrate fertilization.
breedingNotes: Anubias 'Nancon' is propagated by rhizome division: cut the rhizome with a clean blade into segments, each retaining several leaves and some healthy roots, then reattach the divisions to driftwood or other hardscape. The plant is generally sterile in aquaria and seldom flowers under home-aquarium conditions, making vegetative division the only practical method available to most hobbyists.

primary.name: KeepFishKeeping
primary.notes: Provided family (Araceae), parentage (A. barteri var. nana x A. heterophylla), lighting (low to medium), growth (slow to medium), care level (easy/beginner), placement (mid to background), spreading rhizome growth form, and the rhizome-must-not-be-buried husbandry warning.

additional.name: Aquatic Arts
additional.notes: Designated secondary (Buce Plant 404'd at the assigned URL). Cross-checked parentage (gives trade label 'A. barteri Nana x A. afzelii Congensis'), temperature 22-27C, pH 5.5-9.0, low-to-moderate lighting, no CO2 dependence, slow growth, midground-to-background placement, easy care, and the rhizome-attachment requirement. Flagged Panaque plecos as a known leaf-chewer exception.

additional.name: Chichi's Planted Dreams
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing: ships Small (~5 in), Medium (~7 in), Large (~9 in), XL (11+ in), giving an approximate adult height range of 13-28 cm. Confirmed temperature 22-27C, pH 5.5-9.0, slow steady growth, low-to-moderate lighting, no CO2 dependence, midground-to-background placement, and the rhizome-not-buried rule.

additional.name: Wetplants
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Araceae), East Tropical African parent origin, creeping/spreading rhizome growth form, pH 6-8, low-medium lighting, slow growth, easy care, and midground-to-background placement. Attributes the cultivar to aquarist Bob Gasser.

additional.name: Betta Botanicals
additional.notes: Cross-checked parentage (combining resilience of A. barteri 'Nana' with elongated leaf shape of A. afzelii 'Congensis'), low-to-medium lighting, no CO2 requirement, slow-to-medium growth via steady rhizome expansion, midground-to-background placement, propagation by rhizome division, easy care, and tolerance of a wide parameter range.
